article.title: European Commission Cloud Breach Traced to TeamPCP’s Trivy Supply-Chain Compromise
article.summary: CERT-EU says the Europa.eu breach exposed names, emails, and email content after a compromised Trivy update led to AWS access.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

CERT-EU said the European Commission discovered a cloud breach affecting the Europa.eu platform on March 24, 2026, and the Commission said the incident was contained without disrupting the availability of the Europa websites and without affecting internal systems ([European Commission press release](https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/api/files/document/print/en/ip_26_748/IP_26_748_EN.pdf); [CERT-EU](https://cert.europa.eu/blog/european-commission-cloud-breach-trivy-supply-chain)).

## What We Know

- CERT-EU said with high confidence that initial access came through the Trivy supply-chain compromise, which Aqua Security publicly attributed to TeamPCP ([CERT-EU](https://cert.europa.eu/blog/european-commission-cloud-breach-trivy-supply-chain)).
- CERT-EU said about 91.7 GB of compressed data was exfiltrated from the compromised AWS account, including names, email addresses, and email content ([CERT-EU](https://cert.europa.eu/blog/european-commission-cloud-breach-trivy-supply-chain)).
- CERT-EU said the compromised backend supports up to 71 clients, including 42 internal Commission clients and at least 29 other Union entities ([CERT-EU](https://cert.europa.eu/blog/european-commission-cloud-breach-trivy-supply-chain)).
- The Record reported that the stolen data appeared on ShinyHunters' leak site on March 28, and that the group described the material as data from mail servers, documents, contracts, and other sensitive material ([The Record](https://therecord.media/european-commission-cyberattack-teampcp)).
- BleepingComputer reported that the Commission was already investigating when the breach became public, and that the threat actor said the data would be leaked later rather than used for extortion ([BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/european-commission-investigating-breach-after-amazon-cloud-account-hack/amp/)).

## What We Don't Know

- CERT-EU said it has not found signs that the attacker moved laterally into other Commission AWS accounts, even though the stolen key had management rights that could have enabled that path ([CERT-EU](https://cert.europa.eu/blog/european-commission-cloud-breach-trivy-supply-chain)).
- The full downstream impact across Union entities is still being assessed ([CERT-EU](https://cert.europa.eu/blog/european-commission-cloud-breach-trivy-supply-chain)).

## Analysis

This incident shows how a supply-chain compromise can turn a software update channel into a production data-exfiltration path: CERT-EU traced the entry point to a compromised Trivy update, then documented AWS credential abuse and public leakage of the resulting dataset ([CERT-EU](https://cert.europa.eu/blog/european-commission-cloud-breach-trivy-supply-chain)). It also extends [earlier Machine Herald coverage of the Trivy compromise](/article/2026-03/24-trivy-supply-chain-attack-escalates-as-teampcp-hijacks-75-github-action-tags-defaced-aqua-security-repositories-and-spreads-to-npm), but with a new victim and a broader public-sector blast radius ([CERT-EU](https://cert.europa.eu/blog/european-commission-cloud-breach-trivy-supply-chain)).
